speaker: interviewer
text: Paul Kornmehl, tape five, 15th of February, 1996. Paul, you were telling me about joining the Royal Dutch--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brigade Irene--

speaker: interviewee
text: Princess Irene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Princess Irene in Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Canada-- Guelph, Canada. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that part of your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: The commando training I had in Guelph, I think altogether, it lasted three months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To give you an idea, we were marching 40 kilometers and with-- on our back, carrying 40 kilos. In the end of the march-- that's how we were trained-- we had to go under a bridge. Everything was in Dutch, by the way-- under a bridge on a piece off wire to get across it. We learned everything what a commando had to do. It wasn't difficult for us, because I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I joined the-- I joined in Guelph. I think it was in May, 1943. And I left-- June, July-- I think August, 1943, I left for England. The boat-- I saw [INAUDIBLE] are still coming to America. I even missed one boat and by mistake, because they gave me three days. And I saw on that paper four days. And I don't know why that happened. And they wanted to court martial me. That put me a little bit back. But I took the next boat. And we were 40,000 men, women on the boat, going from Canada to England. We arrived in Scotland. From Scotland, they took us by train to London. It was-- everything was dark. And I had to go, coming from Europe-- or escaping from Europe during the wartime, I had to go to the patriotic school. The patriotic school was a school where they interrogated the people or investigated the people, because they were worried about spies. And this didn't have anything to do with your religion. And it just happened that way. I was in the patriotic school, as far as I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] close to two weeks. And then one week, I was in a Dutch patriotic school with a colonel. Again, I don't remember the name. [? I get-- ?] also knowing-- wanted to know exactly how I escape from Holland, my experience in Holland, Belgium, and France, and Spain. So for one week, they kept me there. In the patriotic school, I had the visit of a friend who came also from [INAUDIBLE]. His name was Natan Glatt, G-L-A-T-T. And he came to visit me. How he found out that I'm in England, I don't know. But he was not in the army because he was a scholar. And he was at the, the degree of being a rabbi. That was Natan Glatt. I can also come back to him, but I don't think that fits into the story. After the patriotic school and after being there, I was sent to-- first to Harwich for training, or first-- and then to Dovercourt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] altogether, we were waiting to be sent to Africa. We didn't think of Europe at all. I made the mistake-- or maybe the right thing-- that I thought that Melitta, who was in America, should come to London. So I started working towards this. And indeed, she came together with Mimi Boers to England and worked at the Dutch government. Mimi Boers as well-- she worked also for the Dutch government. Barend Boers, as I mentioned before, became my very close friend. He was also in the Royal Dutch Brigade. And he also had his training in Canada. And it was in the month of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] May, 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the-- we have picked, 32 people, from the Brigade. My captain didn't like me at all. The reason was that three-- he had three officers, one second lieutenant and two first lieutenants, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of which two out of three we helped in Lyons. And he didn't like them. And because he didn't like them, they told him that I did so much for them in Lyons, staying in the Hotel Perrache. And one of them by the name of Sondberchen was a little-- a bit of a schlemazel. And I got him out via [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] three times of prison from [INAUDIBLE] on the [? Sea. ?] He felt that I'm a bad boy because he didn't like these, his officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by not liking me, he appointed me and another guy-- not Barend Boers-- that we should be the first to go-- we were only talking about Africa. We were not talking about Europe-- being what we called [INAUDIBLE]. That means you-- if you have a brigade, we need some people to go first to make space for the infantry, the artillery, you know, for the brigade, you know, whatever they have got.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the 20-- connected with the artillery, which were the 25-pounders. We had eight of them. And they appointed me. Or they, they, they told me that I'm the OP post. OP post stands for Observation Post, which goes in front of the infantry. And they usually pick a site which is a bit high, which could be a church, which could be a mountain. And you direct the 25-pounders, which was the heavy artillery, towards a certain project or subject. If you had this, you only use one of the 25-pounders. And if you find the right [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] project, the right position, then all the other ones can start shooting. And I was with the OP post.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the beginning of May, I was told that we are going-- you know, a few days before-- I was going to Africa. 32 men. And the 32 men-- and I had the photo here, but I haven't got it anymore. It's in Holland-- we were put on a boat. And, and, and I rang Melitta through the government, Dutch government, in London and [? Arlington ?] [? House, ?] and she came. And I told her we are leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was wrong. Again, I did something wrong. And this captain was only looking towards me of doing something wrong. Because I was not supposed to tell anybody, including my wife, that I'm leaving. And I left on the fourth of May for Africa. And I landed D-Day in Normandy with 32 men. We had to look for a position. Now, fortunately, I would say, fortunately, I would say that we landed there. And we arrived there in one piece. Because what the English, Americans, Canadians did there, they landed on a piece of land which-- if I think of it now, 20 miles wide, six to 10 miles deep, one and a half million people. The few Germans which were there-- there were not a few-- they couldn't miss anybody. How many people got lost there, I don't know. But we were not really afraid. We were not worried. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- if somebody died right and left in the center, we thought, these people were not lucky. There wasn't that worry that one could get killed. And I can't explain that. Or I can explain that. Because we were young, inexperienced, and we were not worried about our lives. Apart from this, in a very short time, there came Montgomery, who got through which we call the, the-- we used to call Montgomery Corned Beef, because we got so much corned beef and Spam and dried eggs that we didn't know how much we could eat of that. At the same time, when he came, or he was there, and he said, we are going. I was under Canadian command, not under English command. But Montgomery was the head. And I think he was a very good general. He brought one division after the other. And he got through, because you-- the defense is always a minimum towards the aggressor. And we were the aggressive troops. We got through, the Montgomery got through, after putting one division after the other. That's why we called him corned beef. Once we were through, it wasn't so difficult anymore, because the farmers there were in short supply of corned beef and of Spam. And we exchanged that for Calvados. Once we had Clavados, it wasn't so difficult anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what Calvados is? It's made from, I think from grain or corn. Grain, I think. And if you drink that, you don't feel anything. And that this what most of the army can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, at Normandy, our brigade arrived on the six or eighth of August. And we had put a [? spread ?] out for them. We went with them towards Belgium, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which wasn't too difficult at the time. The counter-offensive of [INAUDIBLE] coming from Germany was rather a surprise to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in Brussels talking to some people. And then I was in Antwerp. And then they put us towards-- close to Arnhem. They took us away from Arnhem to put us to Zeeland. When Arnhem happened, which was a debacle for our division-- I mean, when we took half of Holland, you can say half of Holland, Arnhem was a debacle for-- or lengthened or delayed the war for six months. Because in Arnhem, they did-- know you, must have seen the film called And this was the mistake which they did in Arnhem. We were in Limburg. And we were in Zeeland. And in Zeeland, we were in North [? Beverland. ?] I was stationed in North [? Beverland. ?] And there was a South [? Beverland. ?] North [? Beverland, ?] we had a little boat. The-- North [? Beverland ?] was-- we occupied North [? Beverland. ?] In South [? Beverland, ?] we had the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one night we went over there. And then we are shooting a little bit. And the next night, the Germans came and were shooting a little bit. Unfortunately, I had a German Shepherd. And he was killed there. I don't know what happened to that photo, either. But maybe I can find that later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were not on top of the world. But we knew that North of the Hague, Amsterdam, they were starving. Starving is no expression. I can't explain that. We, we, we got that information through, because you know, we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, I rented a car at a garage in Goes-- G-O-E-S-- from a fellow which was my customer in 1939, 1940. I rented it. He didn't charge me anything for it. And I started exchanging cigarettes for flowers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for milk powder, for all sorts of things. I filled up that garage to the top. It could have been two garages, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had plenty of cigarettes. Because Melitta was working for the Dutch government. She could send me cigarettes. And I didn't sell them. I exchanged that for everything which was necessary when we liberated the north of Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we stayed in North [? Beverland ?] until-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we went to North [? Beverland. ?] I would say what would have been in the months of October, November, 1944. It could have been December. In January, we were there. In February, March, they moved us closer to-- how was that called? Closer to the border of Rotterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we had a bit of a downfall. Some of our guys picking up water and so on, they went on a mine. And they died there. One of my close friends died there, [? Rodriguez. ?] And I have to have some water in between. [DRINKS] And right-- And then we got, we got marching orders. We are going to liberate the north of Holland. And the place where we lost a lot of men, I think-- and I have to check this up if it was right-- I think it was [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]. I have to check this up. We lost a lot of men there. And we had to withdraw. And then they gave me a week furlough, because it was close to the end of the war, to go to London. And that was in the end of April, beginning of May, a week up and down. And I came back. And then we liberated the north of Holland, which was the end of the war. To liberate the Hague, sure, it speaks by itself that I was crying. And the people were hungry ad starving. You know that we had these parachutes coming down with food. We were stationed in the-- in a place around the Hague, which was the, which was the [INAUDIBLE]-- which was-- not [INAUDIBLE]. Yes, it was where they usually put up the meat and all sort of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in charge of the food. We handed out the food as much as possible to everybody. And there arrived-- I wanted to get-- to liquidate these garages in Zeeland . And the, and the bridge-- I'm starting to speak Dutch-- the bridge over the [INAUDIBLE] dike didn't exist anymore, you know, which was bombed or destroyed by the Germans. So I organized three trucks to go to Goes and come back to the Hague. Because in the Hague, I found with the liberation there was this-- the president of, of the synagogue-- not the synagogue, from the Jewish community. It will come to me in a minute-- and a few others, which they call it the center of arriving Jews from other countries or concentration camp. In Dutch, I can say that to you. Should I say it in Dutch? I think I should say that in Dutch. It was-- [SPEAKING DUTCH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in the [INAUDIBLE]. I remember that. And I knew at the time, it was not a matter of having money. Because it was the old Dutch guilder, which wasn't worth a-- wasn't worth-- not worth anything. So with Melitta being with the-- in the Dutch government, I had millions of cigarettes. And that is what I gave them. I said, these are the cigarettes. I never sold one cigarette ever in my life. I gave it to them. By, by the way, Barend Boers, who did some similar things-- because this video is going to be shown to Mimi Boers, who is still alive-- he did not sell one cigarette. On the contrary, I can tell you a story if we come in between. I gave all the cigarettes to the Jewish-Dutch center that when they arrive with cigarettes, you could exchange anything. For myself-- OK. We don't talk about it. We are not there yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where-- there we are. I had to work for this captain which hated me-- he really hated me-- where everybody was able to see his relatives. He said I had to make up reports for him. His name was Rezeeuw. Rezeeuw. Rezeeuw is written R-E-Z-E-E-U-W. Of this video, I'd like to have another copy. And I want to send it to him. He hated me, because everybody was able to see their relatives. And this man kept me there, typing his future, right, to give him the lift which he deserves. He's now a general, by the way, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is what I did. Because I had no choice. He hated me. I can't tell you more about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, so there arrived three trucks from Zeeland which were delivered in the [INAUDIBLE] Straat. I don't know. It was number 10 or eight, but [INAUDIBLE] Straat is a very little street. And it is still in existence there. I delivered everything to give to the people. Not that I am such a good man, because I had everything, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ourselves-- Melitta came by plane, arrived in the month of August to Holland, still being in the Dutch government. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could have afforded to buy a house in the best suburbs in the Hague by giving cigarettes. We went to a house where we paid 15 guilders-- in [INAUDIBLE]-- per month, because we didn't want to buy a house with cigarettes. We didn't want to buy a house with dollars-- 15 guilders a month, which downstairs we were there and upstairs were the NSBers. NSBers were the Nazis in Holland. They had the whole house in [? Fullburg. ?] [? Fullburg ?] was, again, a suburb of the Hague. He was in jail. She told me-- she was a very nice person-- that he's innocent, the usual story. He did not put the people towards the Nazis or anything like that. On the contrary, they put him in also as a [INAUDIBLE] in a very large organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm, you know, wandering away of what I'm telling you. So anyway, I was demobilized in the month of August, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1945. Now, what am I going to do? What am I going to do? Now, there it was. I could do a few things. I went back into hosiery, because we had a quota. And the quota was-- do you know what a quota is? Surely you know. Quota was that I had the right to import a certain quantity, because we were in the business in 1939, 1940. My company in 1939, 1940, was called [? IP Ko. ?] I-P-Kornmehl-- my brother Isaac, Paul, Kornmehl. So we started under the same name. But my brother was still in Israel. And he knew from the newspapers that I was-- that we were very, very poor. So he said, I sent you something in the mail to get you going. And the, the-- and in Israel they had teeth, you know, teeth, artificial teeth, which were very much in demand all over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in Holland they didn't have anything. But teeth was something which exactly they didn't need. But you could still sell it. You could sell anyth-- you could sell anything in Holland. The-- in Holland, the people were starving. And when you went to people, they told you that they were smoking these cigarettes made out of-- they called it [INAUDIBLE] cigarettes-- made out of nothing, of timber.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were also-- there is this story which-- should I tell you the story of Chaim and [? Moshe ?] that came back? No. Your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will not put that in. Because that's got nothing to do with it. Would you like to hear that story?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your story only.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my story. Chaim and [? Moshe ?] came back. Chaim was living in Holland before the war. And [? Moshe ?] was from a concentration-- both from concentration camp, from Auschwitz, Bergen-Belsen. So they went to the first family. And they said, you know, we have been in Bergen-Belsen. So they said, oh, I'm so happy to see you. Isn't it wonderful to have you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Terrific. You know what we went through here? We didn't have anything to eat. We were starving. We were smoking these terrible cigarettes. My husband was driving on the bicycle without tires and so on, just to get some timber and so on. Terrible-- and you know, your diamonds which you left in your [INAUDIBLE]? We had to exchange it for, for some-- something just to keep ourselves alive. So they went to the next place. And Moshe and-- Chaim said, don't worry. There, I put something. I had some gold there and some silver. And there was this woman who said, oh, yes. I'm so happy that you are back, [? Moshe and ?] Chaim. [? Moshe. ?] Chaim, oh, I don't know. I'm so happy that you are back. Isn't that wonderful? Isn't that fantastic you are here? You know what we went through it? My husband was 78 kilos before. He is 42 kilos. And my son who was 50-odd kilos is now only 26 kilos. There's nothing left. We exchanged everything you had for just to keep ourselves going. So [? Moshe ?] said to Chaim, aren't we happy that we were in Auschwitz and Bergen-Belsen? What they went through is so much worse than what we went through. OK. This is my story. And now I go on, right? Where are we? We are there, right? So we are in the Hague. Melitta comes back. We are staying in [? Fullburg. ?] What year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are talking now in August, September, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and I wanted to start a hosiery company again. I didn't have any money. I could have had millions of dol-- millions of guilders. And I didn't want to. I gave all my cigarettes and whatever I had. We lived on 15 guilders a week-- per month in [? Fullburg. ?] I don't want to put myself on the-- but anyway. That is the way it was. So there was a fellow called [? Willy ?] [? Jaeger. ?] He gave me a room where I can have my merchandise. And I went to the distribution company with a quota. And I wasn't in uniform anymore. And they gave me a quota, because I was in 1939, '38. But maybe they gave me the quota because I was Jewish. This I can't tell you. To get the distribution, but we were only allowed to make a profit of 25%. So there it was, 25%. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to be changing the tape now, Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll complete the story shortly.
